author: @kathrynck
text:
I'm wondering if you'd want tubercles nearer the center, and use a more twisted blade... kinda transition from kinetic capture as the water comes into the center, and then with the twist to the blades, transition to something like an impeller fan capture for the water trying to escape the back plate? 
Basically a 90 degree twist (and an increasing curve to the turbine blades) as the water movement transitions from moving perpendicular to the back plate, to horizontal to the back plate (as you progress down the cone shape at the axle. Also maybe a fluted outer cowl too, which matches up with the central cone's contour. 
Actually, kinda wonder if that's good for air as well. (apologies if you've already done this and I missed it 🙂)

author: @wikedawsom
text:
Since it's a propeller design, it seems to be made for taking a large volume of fluid straight-on from the front, rather than a jet at an angle from the side... Maybe submerging in a flowing creek would be a cheap way to test side-by-side with tubercule and non-tubercule props.

Also, i feel like generating hydro power from a rain spout would be a neat application that most people can benefit from, since solar power wouldnt come through on those rainy days.

replies.author: @Nikosi9
replies.text: I also noticed that he didn't didn't aim the hose at the front of the turbine, even though he had a cone in the center...

replies.author: @wikedawsom
replies.text: @Nikosi9 to be fair, I would probably do it the same way he did if I were also using a jet of water, but I just think this turbine design would be very useful for people who want to harvest hydro power in a situation where it doesn't make a lot of sense to terraform a river in order to harvest from it (maybe for temporary energy demand, or other situations where the infrastructure is not as viable)

replies.author: @Nikosi9
replies.text:
@wikedawsom My son lives next to a canal and the tides come and go constantly. There's no real head to speak of. I'm wondering if something like this would produce enough electricity. 
Since the tides are slow and strong, I'm thinking that he might need to use a gearbox of some sort to generate the needed revs for the generator...

replies.author: @wikedawsom
replies.text: @Nikosi9 I agree, you probably would need some kind of gearing or a generator that is otherwise designed for high torque, low speed input

author: @kadmow
text: - that style of turbine - a mild redesign  - into a Francis type runner (that size turbine will need a huge flow rate to fully develop hydro dynamic reaction  flow  (IF the outlet is adjusted, designed to allow free exit of the inlet volume  -as power is extracted velocity reduces, pressure rises - typical Bernoulli principle stuff - in incompressible constrained flows - follow the streamlines)  - the power output could be huge if matched to the correct water source and generator..  cute demo.  - a jet like that as demonstrated  will always generate reaction type responses on impingement, more like a drag type -wind turbine, with freewheeling speed never exceeding inlet speed (velocity if the direction  vector is considered))

author: @Patriarchtech
text: Great video Rob. I work with micro hydro turbines. This clearly works, however in order for this to work as a hydraulic/reaction/pressure turbine and not an Impulse one, it would need to be ducted and completely flooded at least to the point of discharge there are exceptions like the Francis which is sort of both. It would be interesting to see it in a "Kaplan" setup with high flow low head. There is a clear difference in the way you part energy on to it from your wind experiments to hitting it with a jet of water on a couple of blades. A propeller, what this effectively is, is not designed to work this way even though it will, so I suspect it will be relatively inefficient.  My guess is the tubercles will in increase performance in hydro if applied correctly. A lot of research has been put into ship props since they were invented centuries ago, and I reckon if one takes a look at something like bow thrusters(which are almost always ducted and therefore designed to work in a duct) and scale it tubercles could augment the performance in a generator setup.(My dad was a Naval Architect  and taught me a great deal about props btw) The testing part is difficult with micro hydro, Peltons and Turgos meaning impulse turbines are by far the easiest design to work with as a hobbyist IMO. I have built 1kw pressure/reaction turbines and I can tell you building the test rig is easily the most expensive and complicated.

Yes, I really enjoyed this video.

author: @cosmicseekin
text: Interesting that you choose to have the turbine mounted on a solid backplate.  If the waste water could escape more parallel to the shaft, there could be better energy capture.  To go one step further there may be more power capture from directing the water jet directly at the face of the blades.  There again the best jet angle could be completely from the opposite side of the blades at about 45 degrees to the right of the shaft line instead of the left.  Plenty of scope for testing the energy captured from the water stream by changing the direction the water stream is shot at the wheel.

replies.author: @kadmow
replies.text: - 1m^3 of water weighs a tonne, the same air weighs ~1kg, yes the scale factors are a thing. (viscosity x50, viscidity, x X,  speed of sound x3, -  Reynolds numbers  matter,

author: @Dr-Sy
text: Is there a way to put a generator on the rear wheels of a front wheel drive car? So when it is being driven along it will charge through the back wheels, Heck' could even add a motor to the rear wheels so it become an electric and gas fuelled.

replies.author: @taqyon
replies.text: Yes and then add more generators on the front wheels while those are driven by the rear electric motors. And the double win is the back generators will get extra energy from the new front motors wich in turn will add more energy to the front and so on! In short, the moment the car starts to roll, we can supply the planet with energy to spare!

replies.author: @brianfilek36
replies.text:
Adding a motor to the rear wheels of a gas vehicle has already been done -  it's called a hybrid.
Adding generators to the rear wheels of a gas vehicle would allow you to generate electricity as you drive, but the added resistance would make you spend way more on gasoline to go the same distance.

author: @bartronicsecurity
text: For years I always used stepper motors from old CD players and stiffy drives for the generator parts of my projects. Reason  is far less work needed to prepare them (they are literally ready as is without having to remove electronics and soldering wires etc). And secondly they are able to generate a decent power output in volts and current at lower revs.  Better suited to low rpm projects.

author: @William_Hada
text: I am still amazed and baffled that adding rough edges to a blade makes it perform better! My intuition is that, sleeker, smoother , more aerodynamic blade design is preferable.

replies.author: @Absfor30
replies.text: Maybe it's for something to do with reduced drag? Concentrating flow to points instead of "blades/planes"...? 🤷

replies.author: @richiesrestorations4883
replies.text: A serrated knife works better in some cases.

replies.author: @jay90374
replies.text: Much of fluid dynamics seems counterintuitive 🤔

replies.author: @kadmow
replies.text: @Absfor30  - increased wetted surface gives more "skin drag", for "high angle of attack" flows - off-axis /suboptimal entrainment - as is seen under dynamic conditions like the wing wind swinging 30 degrees - which could otherwise lead to separation and dynamic stalling of the blade  - huge induced drag (this can destroy wind turbines) a "leading edge vortex generator" - such as a "tubercle" - or tumor - can "reenergise the flow"  into twin counter rotating vortices which conform to the low pressure surface - Coanda-like-effect...  (These blades are more like a propeller, the shape needs to be altered for all these turbine types being  demonstrated, the simple fact is a propeller will be backdriven by impinging flows, so will a bicycle wheel..  think of the high vs low pressure sides, or even if it matters in the fully "air" ventilated impulse demos.)

replies.author: @stoneomountain2390
replies.text:
Laminar flow is terrible for power transmission.
You need to push, not cut through the fluid. There is a sweet spot though, you don't want to cause cavitation.

Now, if you look at modern ship design, they have a dolphin nose just beneath the waterline.
I suspect it is to draw air along the hull to "lubricate" the front widening areas of the skirt.
